_source: Falco community rules, Sysdig threat research, CNCF security working group
_note: Long-tail Falco rules. Production-tested detection rules beyond the defaults.

falco_rules.description: A shell has been spawned in a container. Legitimate applications should not need interactive shells.
falco_rules.condition: spawned_process and container and proc.name in (shell_binaries) and not (proc.pname in (shell_binaries, container_entrypoints))
falco_rules.output: Shell spawned in container (user=%user.name container=%container.name image=%container.image.repository shell=%proc.name parent=%proc.pname cmdline=%proc.cmdline)
falco_rules.detection_rationale: Attackers use interactive shells after initial foothold; containers should run processes not shells
false_positives: CI/CD build containers that intentionally run scripts
falco_rules.tuning: Add exceptions for CI/CD namespace or debug containers

falco_rules.description: Attempt to read /etc/shadow which contains hashed passwords. No legitimate process needs this at runtime.
falco_rules.condition: open_read and fd.name=/etc/shadow and not proc.name in (allowed_shadow_readers)

falco_rules.description: Network reconnaissance tools executed; may indicate attacker mapping internal network.
falco_rules.condition: spawned_process and proc.name in (network_tools) and not (proc.pname in (authorized_scanners))

falco_rules.description: Write attempt to unexpected directory under root. Attackers write backdoors/webshells here.
falco_rules.condition: write_file and fd.name startswith / and not fd.name startswith /tmp and not fd.name startswith /var/log and not proc.name in (legitimate_writers)

falco_rules.description: Attempt to load kernel module. Rootkits use this to persist and hide.
falco_rules.condition: spawned_process and proc.name in (kmod_tools) and not (proc.pname in (authorized_module_loaders))
falco_rules.output: Kernel module load attempt (user=%user.name module=%proc.args proc=%proc.name)

falco_rules.description: Known cryptocurrency mining software executed. Indicates cloud resource hijacking.
falco_rules.also_detect: High CPU + network to mining pools (pool.minexmr.com etc.)

falco_rules.description: kubectl run inside a container suggests attempted Kubernetes cluster takeover.

falco_rules.rule_name: Outbound Connection to Tor Exit Node
falco_rules.description: Network connection to known Tor exit node IP. Strong indicator of C2 or data exfiltration.
falco_rules.note: Maintain updated Tor exit node list from dan.me.uk/torlist; update daily
falco_rules.output: Tor exit node connection (user=%user.name proc=%proc.name remote=%fd.rip:%fd.rport)

falco_rules.description: High rate of file rename/write operations; potential ransomware encryption activity.
falco_rules.condition: write_file and fd.name pmatch (/home/%, /var/www/%) and count(fd.name) > 100 within 30s
falco_rules.note: Requires Falco with counting support or SIEM aggregation
falco_rules.output: Potential ransomware: high rate file modification (proc=%proc.name user=%user.name)
falco_rules.also_watch: vssadmin.exe delete shadows; bcdedit /set recoveryenabled no

falco_rules.description: SETUID binary executed by non-root user. SUID binaries run with file owner's privileges.
falco_rules.condition: execve and proc.is_suid=true and proc.name not in (known_suid_binaries)
falco_rules.attacker_use: Write SUID shell (/tmp/rootshell with SUID bit); execute to get root

reducing_false_positives: Use Kubernetes namespace exceptions for CI/CD
Whitelist specific process names that legitimately trigger rules
Use priority levels; start WARNING, escalate after tuning
Tag rules by MITRE technique for context
performance_tuning: Avoid broad 'write_file' rules on busy servers; use fd.name filters
Use macros to share condition logic; reduces compilation overhead
Limit output fields; each field lookup has cost
Drop rules not relevant to your environment
